title: Chiefs Offense
content:
# Chiefs Offense Overview

Kansas City continues to lean on motion, spacing, and quarterback improvisation to create explosive plays. The offense is most dangerous when the passing game forces safeties to rotate late and linebackers to communicate through bunch or stack releases.

When defenses sit in soft zone, the short passing game becomes an extension of the run. Quick outs, option routes, and crossers allow the quarterback to stay on schedule while still threatening yards after catch.

The biggest stress point for defenses is handling layered route combinations. If the flat defender widens too quickly, the intermediate window opens. If the defense squeezes inside, the offense takes easy completions outside the numbers.

title: Cover 3 Notes
content:
# Cover 3 Notes

Cover 3 keeps three defenders deep and usually asks four underneath defenders to rally downhill. It is structurally sound against vertical shots, but disciplined offenses can still attack the seams, curl windows, and flats.

Wide receivers who win on deep outs, posts, and seam benders often produce well against Cover 3 looks. Tight ends can also stress hook defenders when route spacing forces the underneath zones to choose between multiple threats.

Defenses that rotate into Cover 3 late can disguise intentions, but communication errors show up quickly when offenses use motion before the snap.

title: Data Bets Og
content:
﻿Mission (do this every time the user asks to analyze a game or slate):
You are a data-only NFL prop analyzer that replicates the DATA BETS three-engine methodology and outputs everything after thoroughly analyzing all data: MGI, GCI (with blowout %), full player projection for each of their respective categories + confidence, and the safest players to choose either over or under on a prop line based on the analyzed data. Do not use betting markets, odds, fantasy projections, or social chatter — only primary team/player data.


Your job is to take this data and analyze it. You choose how this data should be analyzed based off of best judgment and historical accuracies as well as whatever trends you see. 


1) Data Intake (pull these, nothing more, nothing less)


Team (for GCI & baseline):


Points For / Points Against (PF/PA)


Yards per Play (off & def)


Offensive & Defensive Success Rate


Red-Zone TD% (off & def)


Turnover Differential


Plays per Game (pace)


Average Drive Length (plays, yards, time)


Starting Field Position differential


ALL OTHER AVAILABLE DATA THAT YOU SEE, WHICH COULD CONCERN THIS CATEGORY


Trenches (for MGI):


PBWR, RBWR, PRWR, RSWR (ESPN win-rate family)


Pressure Rate (defense)


Sack % allowed (offense)


Rush yards before/after contact (run efficiency)


Player Usage (for Player Module):


Snap %


Routes Run % (for receivers/TEs/RBs)


Targets per Route Run (TPRR)


Target Share % & Touches per Game


Carries per Game


Red-Zone touches/targets


3–4 game trend flags (rising / flat / falling for snap%, routes, targets, carries)


Opponent Splits (for Player Module adjustment):


YPC allowed / YPA allowed


EPA allowed vs RB pass / WR routes / TE routes


Explosive play % allowed (≥20 yds)


Coverage tendency (zone/man %) and completion % allowed by family


Injuries & Continuity (Volatility Guards):


OL starters & continuity (games with same starting 5)


QB / RB1 / WR1 / TE1 status


Key defensive injuries (DL, LB, CB1)


Tag flags: OL_CHANGE, WR1_OUT, RB1_LIMITED, CB1_OUT


Source Policy (must browse):


ESPN team pages & win-rate tables


Pro-Football-Reference (PFR) for game logs & snap/route data


Official injury reports & credible beat writers


Never use: betting/odds sites, DFS/fantasy projection pages, social media rumors


If data conflicts: state the conflict, pick the most credible primary source, and lower confidence


Also collect and analyze:


Pressure vs Protection mapping (team and positional pressure / protection matchups, time-to-pressure differentials).


RB situational usage (carry share by script) — lead/tied/trailing splits and 4Q close-out share.


Explosive-pass context — explosive pass rate and explosive allowed rate by coverage family.


GATHER AND ANALYZE ALL OTHER AVAILABLE DATA THAT YOU SEE, WHICH COULD CONCERN THIS CATEGORY


Freeze Rule:


By default use season-to-date (Weeks 1 → current) as of request time.


If the user asks to “freeze” (e.g., “use only Weeks 1–4”), restrict inputs to that window and label the freeze date in output.


2) Analysis Engines (compute in this order)


A) MGI — Matchup Geometry Index (σ value):
Style-on-style advantage using trenches & coverage.


Combine PBWR vs PRWR, RBWR vs RSWR, sack% allowed vs pressure%, and coverage fit (zone/man) + explosive differential (YPP).


Output: sigma_advantage for each side (e.g., KC +0.4σ) and a short rationale.


B) GCI — Game Control Index (σ value + blowout %):
Expected control using team efficiency & possession drivers.


Inputs: success-rate diff, YPP diff, plays/game, RZ TD% (off/def), turnover margin, drive length, field position.


Output: control_sigma for the likely controller and blowout_risk_pct.


If GCI gap ≥ 0.8σ, activate Blowout Suppression (see Guards).


C) Player Module — Full projections for all primary skill players (QB/RB/WR/TE):


Build median and range from usage (snap/routes/TPRR/targets/carries), red-zone share, trends, and opponent splits.


Prefer whatever stats are viewed as safest based on the data alone (RB rush attempts, WR/TE receptions, QB pass attempts; then RB rush yards, WR/TE receiving yards, RB rush+rec, ETC....).


Output per player: median, low/high range, confidence %, drivers (usage, trenches, coverage fit), and volatility flags.


3) Volatility Guards (apply after projections, then re-compute confidence)


Protection Volatility: If ΔPBWR ≥ 8% or OL_CHANGE, haircut passing props (–10 to –15% confidence) and raise blowout risk by +5–10 pp.


Alpha-Surge: If a WR/TE has a 3-game rising target share, widen reception/yardage range (+1 catch or +15–20 yds) and bump confidence slightly.


Explosive Override (RB): If RB logged ≥2 explosive runs (15+ yds) over last 3, widen rush-yard ceiling (+10–15 yds), do not inflate attempts.


Blowout Suppression: If GCI ≥ 0.8σ, downgrade time-dependent overs (targets/attempts late-game) one tier; prefer attempts/receptions floors over raw yards.


4) Correlation & Safe Pool Rules


Single-game correlation haircut: when all legs come from one game, reduce per-leg confidence by 5–15% (based on overlap/script dependence).


Safe Pool threshold: include only legs with confidence ≥ 0.70 after the haircut.


Respect user platform rules (e.g., “no same-team combos” or “min 2-leg / 3-leg flex”). If user provides no rules, default to:


Multi-game slates → build cross-game pairs.


Single-game slates → use complementary volume legs + one balancing leg (often an UNDER on attempts).


5) Required Output (ALWAYS show everything)


Header summary (plain English): MGI, GCI, blowout risk, key trench notes.


Player Projections Table: each primary skill player with multiple stat lines, and what you project it to be.


Safe Pool (legs ≥0.70 confidence post-haircut): list stat + direction (e.g., “Etienne Rush Attempts OVER 13–17”).


Suggested Combos (respect user rules):


JSON block (see schema) so the user can log or automate.


Notes section: any missing data, conflicts, or guard activations.


6) Backtesting & First-TD (only if user asks)


Backtest mode: Freeze inputs as of date X, produce projections, then fetch the final box score and grade band hits, control direction, and note root-cause tags for any severe miss (OL_CHANGE, SCHEME_SHIFT, BLOWOUT, ROLE_VOLATILITY).


First-TD mode (experimental): rank top 5 scorers by combining early-drive control, red-zone share, and explosive rate; return % estimates and caveats.


7) Tone & Constraints


Be direct and specific. Show numbers and reasoning.


If any key input is missing look harder, you'll find it. 


Never reference Vegas lines, odds, fantasy projections, or social sentiment.


Output JSON Schema (append this block every time)
{
  "game": {
    "home": "",
    "away": "",
    "date": "YYYY-MM-DD"
  },
  "data_freeze": "YYYY-MM-DD (weeks used)",
  "injury_status": {
    "notes": "",
    "risk_flags": ["OL_CHANGE","WR1_OUT","RB1_LIMITED","CB1_OUT"]
  },
  "mgi": {
    "sigma_advantage": 0.0,
    "notes": "brief trench/coverage rationale"
  },
  "gci": {
    "control_sigma": 0.0,
    "blowout_risk_pct": 0,
    "notes": "tempo/plays/red-zone/turnover rationale"
  },
  "players": [
    {
      "name": "",
      "team": "",
      "position": "",
      "stat": "rush_attempts | rush_yards | receptions | receiving_yards | pass_attempts | pass_yards | rush+rec",
      "median": 0,
      "range": [0, 0],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "drivers": ["usage","trenches","coverage_fit","opponent_split","red_zone"],
      "volatility_flags": []
    }
  ],
  "safe_pool": [
    {
      "name": "",
      "team": "",
      "stat": "",
      "direction": "OVER | UNDER",
      "range": [0, 0],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "why": "one-liner"
    }
  ],
  "combos": {
    "two_leg_core": [
      [
        { "name":"", "team":"", "stat":"", "direction":"" },
        { "name":"", "team":"", "stat":"", "direction":"" }
      ]
    ],
    "three_leg_flex": [
      [
        { "name":"", "team":"", "stat":"", "direction":"" },
        { "name":"", "team":"", "stat":"", "direction":"" },
        { "name":"", "team":"", "stat":"", "direction":"" }
      ]
    ]
  },
  "correlation_haircut_applied": true,
  "citations": ["<link1>", "<link2>"],
  "notes": "missing data, conflicts, guard activations, caveats"
}

title: Decision Tree Pt2
content:
﻿Additional High-Value Data to Reduce Variance in NFL WR Receiving-Yards Forecasts
Executive summary
The biggest “consistency gap” in WR receiving-yards forecasting is not box-score randomness—it’s unobserved pregame information about (a) true role (alignment, route intent, matchup), (b) true environment (blocking/pressure, scheme interactions), and (c) collective expert priors (markets and projections) that already synthesize many hidden signals. The highest-impact additions therefore come from market-implied expectations (player props, DFS projections) and film/tracking-derived context (CB matchups, alignment, route concepts, trench win rates), because they directly stabilize “targets and yards-per-target” uncertainty. Most other add-ons (travel, field conditions, officiating) can help at the margins, mainly by improving tail-outcome classification and reducing season-to-season drift.
Why forecasts still wobble with core public data
Even with high-quality play-by-play plus participation-style tables, WR yardage remains volatile because the model often doesn’t know what the coaching staff intends to do pregame, nor what the defense intends to take away. Two games can look identical in the trailing stats but differ materially in route deployment (slot vs wide), “shadow” CB assignment, bracket/double rate, motion usage, and pass-protection feasibility of deep concepts—each of which changes both target probability and target depth/quality.
The most reliably stabilizing information sources are those that either (1) directly observe these hidden variables (film/tracking/charting), or (2) compress many hidden variables into a single number (markets). Notably, official betting-data distributors emphasize real-time distribution of official play-by-play, proprietary tracking outputs, and official betting feeds—an explicit acknowledgment that these feeds are commercially valuable precisely because they are high-signal and timely. 
 All-22 Available for All NFL Fans: How to Use It to Your Advantage 

 PFF WR vs. CB Matchup Chart 

 NFL PLAYER PROP HISTORY & 2023 PREVIEW | Props.Cash 

 2026 Super Bowl Prop Bets: A Primer For The Big Game 

Ranked additional data types that materially improve stability
Assumption: modeling at player–game level for 2016–present, with pregame feature availability where possible.
The table below lists underused or missing data types beyond what you named (nflverse / NGS summaries / play-by-play / participation / snap counts / injuries / weather). “Uplift” is qualitative (High/Medium/Low) in terms of accuracy + stability across seasons, not a guarantee.
Rank
	Additional data type (beyond your stack)
	Expected uplift
	Why it helps (mechanism)
	Typical availability + example sources
	Recommended span & granularity
	Suggested transformations/features
	Integration notes (join/alignment/prep)
	Pitfalls, licensing, and practical proxies
	1
	Closing player receiving-yard props (and receptions / longest reception)
	High
	Market prices aggregate late-breaking role + injury + matchup + weather + scheme info; often the strongest single “prior” for yards. Official distributors explicitly sell an “official sports betting data feed,” indicating structured access exists. 
	Paid/limited (official feeds) vs paid API aggregators. Official: Genius Sports describes the official betting data feed relationship.  Aggregators: The Odds API covers props. 
	2016–present; event-time snapshots (open/close) mapped to player–game
	Use closing line (yards), implied mean; also use receptions, longest catch, alt-line slope; bin into tiers and interact with WR archetype
	Join on game_id + player identity mapping; normalize player names; align to closing time (e.g., kickoff-lock)
	Licensing/ToS risk when scraping sportsbooks; prefer licensed feeds/APIs. Proxy: consensus fantasy projections (multiple sites) or DFS projections if props unavailable. Official licensing context: NFL data licensed to bookmakers via partners. 
	2
	Prop line movement (open→close deltas; volatility; “steam”)
	High
	Movement captures information arrival (late injury news, role leaks, matchup shifts). Often improves consistency more than adding another football stat.
	Same as above (requires time series odds). APIs like The Odds API explicitly support player props via event endpoints. 
	2016–present; multiple timestamps per game
	Δline (close–open), intraday rolling volatility, #revisions; “sharp move” flags
	Align timestamps to a common timezone; de-duplicate books; enforce “no future” cutoff
	High missingness across books/markets; survivorship bias in which props are posted. Proxy: compare early-week fantasy projection vs late-week projection deltas.
	3
	DFS projection feeds (and, when available, salaries/ownership)
	High
	DFS projections are another market prior; they may stabilize target expectations and role, especially for WR2/WR3 where usage is fragile. FanDuel Research explicitly offers exportable projection tables (“Download as CSV”). 
	Public/free-to-view in some places; some ownership tools are paid. Example: FanDuel Research projections pages. 
	2016–present; weekly player projections snapshots
	Use projected fantasy points → implied yards proxy; derive “projection rank on team”; if salary available, use salary-implied opportunity
	Join by player name/team/week; store projection timestamp
	Scraping restrictions are explicit in ToS; automation/scraping can violate terms.  Proxy: freely available consensus rankings/projections (less stable but legal).
	4
	Licensed official league data feeds (richer IDs; consistency; sometimes additional event metadata)
	Medium–High
	Even if you already have pbp, licensed feeds can reduce data drift and improve ID alignment; some include richer timing/state fields useful for stable feature engineering. Official distributors highlight real-time official play-by-play + tracking + betting feeds distribution. 
	High (enterprise) via official distributors/partners (e.g., Genius Sports; Sportradar has NFL relationships in public reporting). 
	2016–present; play-level and event-level feeds
	Prefer feed IDs as canonical keys; derive stable game state and possession features; use feed timestamps to align with props/news
	Build an ID crosswalk layer (GSIS/game_id/player_id); monitor schema changes
	Contract/licensing complexity; may limit redistribution. Proxy: keep nflverse as base, but implement strict schema-versioning and QA checks.
	5
	Full-resolution frame-level tracking (routes, separation time series, leverage, scramble drill)
	High (if accessible)
	Raw tracking enables you to measure why a WR exceeded (separation early, motion-created leverage) rather than only outcomes; improves stability by modeling underlying process. The NFL publishes tracking data subsets via Big Data Bowl competitions on Kaggle. 
	Limited public subsets (Big Data Bowl), otherwise high-cost licensing
	Best effort: 2018+ subsets depending on release; frame-level (10Hz), aggregated to player–game
	Pre-snap motion rate, separation-at-break, route stem speed, time-to-open; build “expected target” models conditioned on getting open
	Need consistent player IDs (GSIS), play_id alignment, and frame-to-play joins; heavy compute
	Big Data Bowl data is not full-season general-purpose; results may not generalize. Proxy: use any available separation metrics (even vendor-separated) or aggregated charting. 
	6
	Access to All-22 Coaches Film for every game (for computer vision or manual charting)
	Medium–High
	Film provides ground truth for alignment, route concepts, bracket coverage, and “designed touches” that are hard to infer from pbp alone. NFL+ Premium states Coaches Film availability for every preseason/regular/postseason game (US). 
	Subscription (consumer), but not a data feed; usable for internal analysis
	2022–present is emphasized in some league product messaging; per-game film segments
	Chart: slot rate, press coverage faced, bracket/double, designed screens, GTY (go-to-yardline) usage; distill to robust bins
	Align film-derived tags to game_id and player id; ensure charting consistency across raters/models
	Strong licensing/redistribution constraints; treat as internal-only. Proxy: buy charting outputs from specialized vendors (below), or chart a limited sample to train a model. 
	7
	Premium charting: WR/CB matchups, assignments, separation (All-Coverage type datasets)
	High
	Shadow coverage and assignment-based separation reduce the “unknown matchup” variance; very useful for explaining under/over performances. PFF describes an All-Coverage dataset including matchups, assignments, separation since 2019. 
	Paid/enterprise; example: Pro Football Focus markets comprehensive data services. 
	2019–present for certain matchup datasets; snap/route-level aggregated to player–game
	Shadow rate; avg separation vs primary CB; expected yards allowed by CB; interaction: WR depth profile × CB style
	Requires vendor IDs; build mapping to GSIS; align to week/game
	Subscription may not include API access; PFF notes API access is not part of consumer subscription and suggests contacting B2B.  Proxy: approximate “shadow” using defense’s typical CB alignment + WR alignment, but it’s weaker.
	8
	Premium charting: route trees, alignments, motion, bunch/stack, concept tags
	Medium–High
	Route concept and alignment define target depth and probability; bunch/stack and motion change separation and coverage leverage, stabilizing predictions across defenses.
	Paid. Vendors like TruMedia Networks and Sports Info Solutions position products around advanced football analytics/queryable play data. 
	Prefer 2016–present; play-level tags aggregated to player–game
	Slot%/wide%; motion rate; screen rate; “designed touch” rate; route-depth buckets by alignment
	Vendor IDs mapping; ensure concept taxonomies are consistent year to year
	SIS DataHub Pro terms restrict redistribution and bulk publishing; treat usage as internal modeling.  Proxy: build coarse tags from limited film charting or from readily available public descriptions (lower reliability).
	9
	Offensive and defensive trench win-rate metrics (PBWR/PRWR)
	Medium–High
	Pass protection vs pass rush drives time-to-throw and route development; deep-yardage outcomes are especially sensitive. ESPN documents PBWR/PRWR definitions (2.5s threshold) based on tracking. 
	Public summary tables/articles; full underlying likely paid/licensed
	2016–present; team and personnel-level weekly; aggregate to game
	Use team PBWR, opponent PRWR; create “deep-route viability” flags; interaction with WR deep aDOT profile
	Join by team/week; avoid postgame leakage—use rolling or prior-week values
	Public availability can be patchy (articles, not clean tables). Proxy: pressure rates derived from pbp (already in your stack) or vendor OL grades.
	10
	Vendor pass-blocking / OL grades and OL unit health/continuity
	Medium
	OL performance affects whether WRs can convert air yards; continuity and injuries matter beyond listed injury tags.
	Often paid (PFF, SIS, others). PFF describes detailed grading by facet. 
	2016–present; player-week or unit-level; aggregate to game
	OL unit grade; continuity index (#returning starters); “backup tackle starting” flags
	Needs OL starter identification; can be derived from gamebooks (see below) or vendor lineup data
	Licensing; differences in grading scales across vendors. Proxy: use pressure allowed, sack rates, and QB time-to-throw tendency (if available).
	11
	Official gameday inactives and late lineup status (beyond injury report labels)
	Medium
	The final inactive list and last-minute role changes swing WR3/WR4 usage and can explain “mystery zeros.” NFL publishes inactives by week. 
	Public/official
	2016–present; game-level released ~90 minutes pre-kick
	Encode WR/QB/OL/CB inactive flags; teammate-absence-driven target delta priors
	Join by team/game_id; parse names reliably; timestamp features as “known pregame”
	Historical inactives need scraping/archiving; ensure ToS-compliant collection. Proxy: use gamebook inactives section where accessible. 
	12
	Official transactions and roster churn (call-ups, IR moves, coaching changes)
	Medium
	Transactions capture role changes before they show in targets; roster churn is a leading indicator for routes and snap share. NFL provides a transactions hub. 
	Public/official
	2016–present; daily transaction-level aggregated to pregame state
	Build “days since activated,” “recent call-up,” “recent trade/signing,” “teammate IR opened targets”
	Requires player ID mapping; reconcile name changes; use transaction dates to prevent leakage
	Transaction logs can be verbose; requires rules to interpret impact. Proxy: depth chart aggregators (less official) or beat reports.
	13
	Official gamebooks (PDF/XML) for structured per-game context (starters, officials, notes)
	Medium
	Gamebooks provide authoritative starters and official game metadata; key for OL/CB starter identification when other sources are noisy. NFL indicates gamebooks are available in game centers; NFL GSIS documentation confirms gamebook composition. 
	Public/official access to PDFs; XML exists for organizations per GSIS help
	2016–present; game-level documents
	Parse starting lineups, inactive lists, and possibly officials; create “starting CB present” and “OL starter missing” flags
	Join by game_id; PDF parsing requires robust extraction; maintain versioned parsers
	PDFs are brittle to parse; XML access may not be public for all users. Proxy: use reputable roster starters datasets (but mind licensing).
	14
	Field surface condition/quality and stadium operations beyond basic weather
	Low–Medium
	Field conditions can change footing, route timing, injury likelihood, and play selection; effects are often nonlinear (worst fields matter most). NFL Football Operations publishes surface best-practices and mandatory practices. 
	Limited public metrics; standards docs are public, measurements often internal
	2016–present; game-level (field type) plus any available condition signals
	Flag turf/grass type + known field-change events; if condition grades exist, bin into red/yellow/green
	Requires stadium mapping and tracking of field replacements; may need manual curation
	Often no consistent public “field condition score.” Proxy: roof/surface type (if already in your pbp) + recent rain + anecdotal reports (noisy).
	15
	Travel distance / time-zone / circadian alignment features
	Low
	Adds context for performance variance, especially in extreme travel/time changes; evidence is mixed and often smaller than markets/role, but can help calibration and tail errors. Older NFL-focused studies evaluate time zone/game time impacts; broader circadian work exists. 
	Public (derived)
	2016–present; game-level derived from team city/stadium coordinates + kickoff time
	Travel miles, time-zone delta, kickoff local time vs body time; short-rest + travel interactions
	Must compute from geocoding (stadiums/teams) and schedule; handle neutral-site games
	Effects can be confounded and team-specific. Proxy: simple time-zone delta + days rest + international game indicator.
	Prioritized acquisition plan with effort and cost categories
A practical approach is to add one high-signal “prior” layer first (markets), then one high-signal “mechanistic” layer (matchup/film/trench), while keeping everything joinable to your existing player–game table.
1. Acquire closing player props (and line-movement history) through an authorized channel (effort: medium; cost: medium–high). If enterprise access is realistic, prioritize an official distributor relationship; the NFL’s official betting/feed distribution ecosystem is explicitly commercialized through partners. 
2. Add a second market prior via DFS projections (effort: low–medium; cost: free–low). Use exportable projection tables where available and store snapshots with timestamps; treat ToS limitations as non-negotiable. 
3. Add matchup/assignment charting (WR/CB) and alignment/route tree tags (effort: medium; cost: high). Vendor charting (PFF/SIS/TruMedia) is often the quickest route to usable structured outputs; PFF describes matchup/assignment/separation collection since 2019. 
4. Add trench win-rate features (effort: low; cost: free–low if using public summaries; medium if licensing). PBWR/PRWR definitions are documented and can be used as team-level stabilizers for deep WR outcomes. 
5. Operational robustness layer: archive official inactives, transactions, and gamebooks for “last-mile” role corrections (effort: medium; cost: free). These are official sources for availability/role signals that frequently explain outliers. 
Mermaid timeline for acquisition and validation
Week 1-2Add storage layer(snapshotted inputs,timestamps, IDcrosswalk QA)Week 2-4Integrate marketpriors (player propsclose + movement;DFS projections)Week 4-7Validate uplift viaablations (holdoutseasons; stabilitychecks)Week 6-10Addmatchup/charting(WR-CB,alignment/routetree, concept tags)Week 10-12Add trench layer(PBWR/PRWR, OLindicators) + re-runvalidationWeek 12-14Add operationallayer(inactives/transactions/gamebooks)+ monitoringData acquisition and validation roadmap (player-game, 2016–present)
Show code
Experiments to validate each data type’s value
The goal is to quantify not just accuracy, but consistency: reduced error variance across seasons/teams and fewer catastrophic misses. Time-aware evaluation is essential because both football and data providers drift year to year.
Core evaluation metrics
Use both regression and classification views on the same predictions:
* Regression (yards): MAE (primary), RMSE (tail sensitivity), and “top-decile miss rate” (how often you miss badly on the biggest-yard games).
* Classification (greatly exceed / meet / fall below): macro-F1, balanced accuracy, and calibration (Brier score or reliability curves) when you produce probabilities.
Ablation design
Run a standardized ladder of models, always using the same time splits, then measure marginal contribution of each data type:
* Baseline: your current feature set (public pbp/participation-derived pregame features).
* Add group Gk: one new data layer at a time (e.g., props; DFS projections; WR/CB matchups; trench win rates; operational/inactives).
* Report: ΔMAE, Δmacro-F1, and change in year-to-year variance of MAE.
A best practice is to test both (a) a strong boosted-tree benchmark and (b) your intended interpretable tree, because some data types help mostly through nonlinear interactions.
Recommended per-item validation experiments
Use these targeted tests to avoid false positives from correlated priors:
* Player props (Rank 1) and movement (Rank 2):
   * Experiment: Add closing line only, then add open/close delta and volatility.
   * Checks: performance with “market only” vs “market + football” to confirm football features still matter; evaluate tail recall (exceed/fall-below).
   * Sample need: at least 3–4 seasons of props coverage (props are increasingly available in recent years). 
* DFS projections (Rank 3):
   * Experiment: Add projections alone; then add “projection change vs prior week” and (if available) salary.
   * Checks: does it reduce misses for low-volume WRs? Compare performance by WR usage tier. 
* WR/CB matchups and assignment/separation charting (Rank 7):
   * Experiment: Add only “shadow rate + CB quality” features; then add separation-based matchup features.
   * Checks: subgroup performance vs man-heavy teams or elite corners; measure stability (lower variance of errors). 
* Alignment/route tree/concept tags (Rank 8):
   * Experiment: Add alignment (slot%) first, then route-depth buckets, then concept/motion tags.
   * Checks: improvement in predicting aDOT-driven variance and deep-shot games. 
* Trench win rates (Rank 9) and OL quality (Rank 10):
   * Experiment: Add team PBWR/opponent PRWR; then add OL continuity/backup-starter flags from gamebooks.
   * Checks: improvement primarily on deep WRs (high aDOT) and vs strong rush teams. 
* Operational signals (Ranks 11–13):
   * Experiment: Add inactives only; then add transactions; then add gamebook-derived starters.
   * Checks: reduction in “mystery zeros” and unexpected snap/route collapses; evaluate classification performance for “fall below.” 
* Field quality and travel/circadian (Ranks 14–15):
   * Experiment: Add only extreme-condition flags and interactions; avoid expecting broad uplift.
   * Checks: calibration improvement and tail suppression (fewer extreme over-predictions). 
Integration and licensing pitfalls that commonly derail “better data” projects
The highest-value new layers (markets, premium charting, film/tracking) are also the most likely to trigger legal, licensing, and operational issues if handled casually. Sportsbook and DFS platforms often include explicit prohibitions on automated collection and scraping, which means a production pipeline should use authorized APIs/licensed providers rather than ad-hoc scraping. 
Similarly, premium charting platforms can contractually restrict redistribution and bulk extraction; for example, SIS DataHub Pro terms explicitly limit redistribution and even the number of data points publishable per piece of content, signaling that you should treat these sources as internal modeling inputs unless you have a broader commercial license.  PFF also explicitly distinguishes consumer subscriptions from organizational API-style access. 
Operationally, the most common technical failure mode is ID mismatch and timestamp leakage. If you add props or projections, store them with immutable timestamps and enforce “known by kickoff” cutoffs; if you add vendor data, allocate time for a robust crosswalk between vendor player IDs and GSIS-style IDs and for season-to-season schema drift audits. When ideal data is unavailable, the most practical proxy stack (in priority order) is: (1) DFS projections, (2) public matchup charts/coverage tendencies from reputable analysts (lower fidelity), (3) limited manual film charting for model training, and (4) engineered interaction features using only your baseline sources—recognizing that proxies are often less stable than the true signals.

title: Decision Tree
content:
﻿Predictive Variables for Forecasting NFL Wide Receiver Receiving Yards
Executive summary
Forecasting a wide receiver’s next-game receiving yards is, in practice, forecasting (a) opportunity and (b) yards per opportunity, then adjusting for (c) game environment and (d) defensive interaction. The best-performing feature sets therefore concentrate on (1) who is on the field and running routes, (2) how targets are distributed, and (3) how deep those targets are, with context features acting as second-order corrections.
Across modern public data (2016+), the most predictive families are:
* Expected opportunity (dominant): route participation (or pass-play participation), target share, and team-level dropbacks/pass intent. These features are available via play-by-play and participation datasets (players on field, formations/personnel) and allow you to predict targets rather than use realized targets (avoids leakage). 
* Depth and “yardage potential”: receiving air yards, aDOT/average air distance, and air-yards share (often combined into composite opportunity metrics like WOPR). 
* Efficiency and separation/YAC context: catchability/accuracy (QB), and receiver separation / expected YAC features provide incremental lift, especially to distinguish “meet vs greatly exceed” outcomes among similarly targeted receivers. 
* Game environment and market expectations: pregame spread/total (and derived implied team points / expected script) is a robust “information aggregator” that often carries hidden injury/scheme/weather signals. These fields are directly available in the nflverse schedule file (spread_line, total_line, moneylines, rest days). 
* Opponent defense: pressure and coverage tendencies (man/zone, coverage shells) and pass efficiency allowed add value, and—crucially for a decision tree—create highly interpretable splits (“vs Cover 3 heavy teams”, “vs high-pressure defenses”). 
* Availability (injuries) and weather: injury status/practice participation and wind/temperature/roof are typically smaller effects than volume and script, but matter for tail outcomes (big misses and big spikes), and help stabilize your “greatly exceed / fall below” classes. 
A practical workflow for “large decision tree classification” is to (1) build a high-accuracy boosted tree to identify the strongest variables, then (2) train a single pruned tree (or a tree distilled from the boosted model) using the top features for interpretability, with cost-complexity pruning to control overfitting. 
Data sources, time span, and granularity
A modern, high-signal public stack is built around the nflverse data releases plus official NFL pages. This stack is strong because it includes play-level participation and coverage labels, not just box scores. 
Recommended core datasets
Play-by-play (1999–present; play-level → game-level aggregates)
* nflverse play-by-play is available back to 1999 and can be loaded for all seasons; it includes key pass concepts like air_yards and yards_after_catch, plus EPA/WP fields that help derive team/pass efficiency context. 
Schedules + betting lines + rest (game-level; includes spread/total):
* The schedule file includes spread_line, total_line, moneylines, and rest fields (away_rest/home_rest), which are extremely useful “game script priors.” 
Next Gen Stats weekly player stats (2016–present; week-level with week==0 season summary):
* Weekly receiving includes targets, yards, avg_air_distance (aDOT), avg_cushion, avg_separation, catch_percentage, avg_expected_yac, and avg_yac_above_expectation; passing includes expected completion and completion_percentage_above_expectation (CPOE). 
Participation + route + coverage labels (2016–present; play-level):
* Participation data includes players_on_play/offense_players, offensive formation/personnel, defender counts/pressure indicators, and critically route, defense_man_zone_type, and defense_coverage_type. 
* This directly supports many of the “scheme / coverage / route participation” features you listed, using public data.
Injury reports (2009–present; weekly; practice participation + game status):
* The injury dataset includes report_status and practice_status, letting you model “questionable + limited” differently than “full participant,” which is aligned with league reporting structure. 
Snap counts (2012–present; game-level):
* Snap counts are sourced from Pro Football Reference and made available in nflverse as game-level offensive/defensive/ST snaps and percentages. 
Official / primary NFL sources that pair well with the above
* NFL injury reporting rules and timing: the injury report policy describes how practice participation is reported and why listed statuses exist. 
* Next Gen tracking technology overview: explains RFID tracking infrastructure used to generate tracking-derived stats. 
* Official current injury lists: NFL injuries page provides the “live” view for the current week (useful for building your real-time pipeline). 
* xYAC definition and intent: explains expected YAC as a tracking-informed expectation baseline. 
Weather sources
* Primary weather archive (recommended for modeling): NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information “Global Hourly” / Integrated Surface Database provides hourly observations at thousands of stations. 
* Aviation METAR feeds: useful for stadium-near airport observations; best for recent/historical lookups. 
Best-practice time span and granularity choices
If you want maximum predictive accuracy with the richest public features, use:
* Time span: 2016–present (unifies Next Gen weekly stats + participation/coverage + play-by-play). 
* Primary modeling table: player-game level (one row per WR per game), with features computed from:
   * play-level participation (routes, coverages, on-field presence) 
   * game-level snap counts, schedule lines, weather summaries 
   * week-level Next Gen receiving/passing stats (joined by week/season) 
If you must go back to 1999 for sample size (e.g., for era-scale models), you’ll lose several high-signal columns (NGS, participation routes/coverage, snap counts), so accuracy is usually lower even with more rows. 
Preprocessing and feature engineering approach
Define the prediction task precisely to prevent leakage
For “will a receiver exceed vs meet vs fall below their average,” define:
* Target: next-game receiving yards (regression) or a 3-class label derived from next-game yards.
* Baseline “average yards”: must be computed using only games prior to the predicted game (e.g., rolling last 8 games, season-to-date excluding current week).
* Example classing (interpretable and stable):
   * greatly exceed: yards ≥ 1.40 × baseline
   * meet: 0.70–1.40 × baseline
   * fall below: yards ≤ 0.70 × baseline
This ratio label keeps the tree’s splits meaningful across WR tiers (stars vs role players) and reduces heteroskedasticity. (The exact thresholds should be tuned for class balance.)
Join keys and “single source of truth” IDs
A repeatable pipeline hinges on consistent IDs:
* Use the nflverse players table as the crosswalk for GSIS and other IDs (“single source of truth” design). 
* Standardize to:
   * game_id (nflverse game key) 
   * play_id for play-level joins (pbp ↔ participation) 
   * player_gsis_id / gsis_id for player joins across injuries, NGS, participation, and pbp. 
Core preprocessing steps
* Filter to “meaningful WR games” to reduce noise: e.g., WRs with ≥10 offensive snaps or ≥5 routes/pass-play participations (computed from participation), and ensure the player is listed as WR in at least one source. 
* Handle structural missingness explicitly (tree models like explicit “missing” bins):
   * NGS stats only exist for players above minimum attempts thresholds (do not impute as zero; encode “NGS_missing”). 
   * Participation/coverage labels differ by season provider; encode “provider/era” as a feature to let the tree learn regime shifts. 
* Weather: play-by-play contains a weather string that can be missing/inconsistent; if weather matters, prefer NOAA station-based values (wind/temp/precip) and aggregate to kickoff ± in-game window. 
Feature engineering patterns that work well for trees
* Shift everything by 1 game/week (or by date) so no future info leaks into predictors.
* Use rolling windows at multiple half-lives: last 3, last 8, and season-to-date (trees often split differently early vs late season).
* Opponent-adjusted deltas: e.g., (WR target share last 8) − (opponent target share allowed to WRs last 8).
* Composite opportunity metrics: WOPR combines target_share and air_yards_share into one opportunity score; RACR captures conversion of air yards to receiving yards. 
* Route/coverage interaction features (highly interpretable splits): e.g., “WR slant rate” vs “opponent man rate,” since participation data includes both route and coverage labels. 
Ranked table of the top predictive variables
The ranked features below are designed to be available pregame by using trailing/rolling summaries (not same-game targets), while still capturing the strongest drivers of receiving yards. Most can be built from play-by-play + schedules + participation + NGS, with injuries and snap counts adding availability context. 
Rank
	Variable (player-game)
	Why it is highly predictive
	Suggested transformations for a decision tree
	1
	Expected targets (next game)
	Targets are the most direct “opportunity” driver; predicting targets avoids leakage and stabilizes yard forecasts.
	Build a targets model: trailing target_share × expected team dropbacks. Use EWMA(3/8) target_share; cap outliers; add teammate-absence adjustments.
	2
	Pass-play participation / route participation
	If the WR isn’t on the field on dropbacks, yards ceiling collapses. Participation data lists offense_players per play. 
	From participation: % of team dropbacks where WR is in offense_players; compute last 3/8 games; add “starting vs rotational” bins.
	3
	Target share (rolling)
	Decouples player usage from game pace; also robust across opponents.
	EWMA target_share; compute slope (last 3 minus last 8); include intra-season rank on team. WOPR uses target_share. 
	4
	Receiving air yards (rolling)
	Captures downfield usage; air_yards is defined at target point and drives yard potential. 
	EWMA receiving_air_yards; split into short/deep share using aDOT bins; include boom-bust indicator (high air yards variance).
	5
	Air-yards share (rolling)
	Team-context normalization for deep usage; materially improves “exceed vs meet” classification. WOPR uses air_yards_share. 
	EWMA air_yards_share; compute WOPR; add “high share + low RACR” (positive regression candidates) split. 
	6
	aDOT / avg air distance
	Separates high-volume shallow roles from lower-volume deep threats; affects yard distribution and variance. 
	Use NGS avg_air_distance or pbp air_yards/targets; bin into shallow/medium/deep; include interaction with expected targets.
	7
	Team expected dropbacks / pass attempts
	Team volume is a hard ceiling/floor for WR yardage.
	Predict team dropbacks using trailing pace + PROE-like intent (xpass-based) + spread/total; include bins for high/low tempo. 
	8
	Game total line (over/under)
	Strong prior for overall scoring and play volume; often embeds injuries/weather in market pricing. total_line is available in schedules. 
	Use total_line; derive implied team points; also include “high-total close spread” vs “low-total blowout” categories.
	9
	Point spread / win probability proxy
	Game script shifts pass volume and late-game target patterns. spread_line exists in schedules. 
	Use spread_line plus bins: favorite/underdog by tiers; interact with team pass intent.
	10
	Receiver efficiency: yards per target / yards per route
	Separates “empty targets” from real yard production; helps among similarly targeted players.
	Rolling yards/target and (yards ÷ pass-play participations). Add regression-to-mean: shrink to league avg by sample size.
	11
	RACR (receiving_yards ÷ receiving_air_yards)
	Measures conversion of air yards into receiving yards; helps predict whether deep usage turns into actual yards. 
	Compute RACR over last 8; winsorize extremes; interact with aDOT and QB accuracy metrics. 
	12
	Expected YAC and YAC above expectation
	Distinguishes receivers who create after catch vs those dependent on depth alone; NGS provides expected YAC and above expectation. 
	Use avg_expected_yac and avg_yac_above_expectation; bin “high separation + high YACAE” archetypes.
	13
	QB accuracy / CPOE-type metric
	Receiver yard outcomes depend on catchable volume; NGS provides expected completion and completion above expectation. 
	Rolling QB completion_percentage_above_expectation; include QB aDOT; interaction with WR aDOT (deep accuracy matters more).
	14
	Opponent pass defense efficiency allowed
	Defensive ability strongly affects completion/yard efficiency; pbp contains EPA per play. 
	Rolling defensive EPA allowed per dropback; explosive pass rate allowed; opponent-adjust WR expected yards/target down.
	15
	Opponent pressure rate + QB time-to-throw context
	Pressure changes depth and target timing; participation includes was_pressure and time_to_throw. 
	Compute opponent pressure rate (last 8), QB time_to_throw (last 8), and interaction bins (slow QB vs high pressure).
	16
	Opponent man/zone and coverage shell rates
	Directly affects route success and target selection; participation includes man/zone and coverage type. 
	Opponent % man, % Cover_3, % Cover_1, etc; create matchup flags (WR role vs opponent shell).
	17
	Receiver route mix / archetype
	Route distribution predicts depth and target type; participation includes route label set. 
	Last-8 route shares (e.g., screen/slant/go/dig); compress via PCA-like buckets or simple “short vs intermediate vs deep route rate.”
	18
	High-value target indicators (red zone / end zone usage)
	A WR can “exceed” on a few high-leverage deep or end-zone looks; helps classify tail outcomes.
	Build red-zone target rate and end-zone target rate from pbp field position, rolling window; interact with team implied points.
	19
	Injury/practice participation status (WR + QB + key teammates)
	Availability and effectiveness hinge on practice status and game status reporting. 
	Encode practice_status (DNP/LP/FP) and report_status; build teammate-absence features for target competition changes. 
	20
	Weather + roof/venue conditions
	Wind/cold shift teams toward conservative passing; effects are strongest at extremes. NOAA provides hourly wind/temp; indoor roofs stabilize. 
	Use kickoff wind speed bins (e.g., <10 / 10–15 / >15 mph), temp bins, precip flag; add roof/dome indicator; interact with aDOT/deep rate.
	Notes on interpretability: the “coverage + route mix” features (ranks 16–17) are particularly valuable for a large decision tree, because they produce split rules that coaches/analysts immediately understand (“man-heavy defense + high slant rate + high expected targets”). 
Tree-based models and feature-importance methods
Models to consider
A single large decision tree is easy to communicate, but it is also prone to overfitting; controlling complexity is essential. 
* CART decision tree (classification/regression): start here for interpretability; tune max_depth, min_samples_leaf, and use cost-complexity pruning (ccp_alpha) to keep the tree stable out-of-sample. 
* Random Forest / ExtraTrees: strong for feature screening and nonlinearity, less interpretable globally. (Often useful to identify that “expected targets” dominates before you build the final big tree.)
* Gradient-Boosted Trees (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost): typically the highest accuracy on tabular sports data; use these to (a) baseline your attainable performance, and (b) rank features before distilling into a single tree. Feature importance can be summarized via “gain / cover / frequency.” 
Feature importance and explanation methods
For robust “most predictive variable” claims, use multiple importance views:
* Permutation importance (model-agnostic): measures score drop when a feature is shuffled. 
* Tree gain importance (boosted trees): e.g., XGBoost’s Gain, Cover, Frequency. 
* SHAP values (global + local): provides consistent per-feature attribution and is well-suited to tree ensembles. 
One caveat: permutation importance can behave misleadingly under strong multicollinearity (common here: targets, target_share, WOPR, routes are correlated). Plan to group correlated features or evaluate drop-column importance to avoid “all features look unimportant.” 
Validation strategy for credible out-of-sample performance
Splitting strategy
Because the NFL is non-stationary (scheme trends, season-to-season rule emphasis, roster turnover), use time-aware validation:
* Holdout seasons (recommended default): Train on 2016–2022, validate on 2023–2024, final test on 2025 (example). This aligns with modern data availability (NGS and participation since 2016). 
* Rolling-origin backtesting: multiple folds where each fold trains on seasons ≤Y and tests on Y+1 (or a block). This reduces the risk that your results depend on one unusual season.
Cross-validation details that matter for WR yards
* Group-aware evaluation: if you evaluate at player-game level, ensure the model isn’t effectively memorizing a player’s season via leakage. Use player-season grouping for certain CV analyses (or at least ensure strict chronological feature shifting).
* Metrics:
   * Regression: MAE (business-friendly), RMSE (penalizes big misses), and calibration plots of predicted vs actual quantiles.
   * 3-class classification: macro-F1 (balances classes), balanced accuracy, and probability calibration (Brier score / reliability curves) if you will use probabilities.
Baselines to include
* “Rolling average only” baseline: predict next-game yards as trailing 8-game mean.
* “Targets-only” baseline: predicted targets × trailing yards/target.
If your fancy model doesn’t beat these by a meaningful margin, you likely have leakage or data quality issues.
Pitfalls and limitations to expect
   * Leakage via same-game opportunity: realized targets, routes, snap counts, and in-game script are outcomes, not predictors. If you include same-game targets you will inflate accuracy artificially. Participation data is play-level; treat it as historical-only for pregame models. 
   * NGS availability thresholds: weekly NGS stats only appear for players above minimum attempts; missingness is informative and should be modeled, not naively imputed. 
   * Provider/era inconsistency: participation data source switches (NGS earlier, FTN Data for 2023+), so distributions of route/coverage labels can shift. Encode season/provider to reduce silent drift. 
   * Weather data quality: play-by-play may include a single weather string that can be missing or inconsistent; using NOAA station data is more defensible, but requires careful stadium-station matching and time-window aggregation. 
   * Collinearity and “importance inflation/deflation”: targets, target_share, WOPR, and routes are tightly coupled; impurity- and permutation-based importance may split credit unpredictably. Use grouped importance and SHAP interaction views. 
   * Small samples for route/coverage matchups: receiver performance vs a specific coverage shell or route type can be sparse; trees will happily overfit unless you enforce min_samples_leaf and prune. 
   * Market lines as “too good”: spread/total improves accuracy partly because it aggregates hidden info. That’s useful, but it can also mask whether your football features are genuinely predictive; report performance with and without market features. 
   * Changing offensive roles mid-season: trades, injuries to other receivers, and coaching changes can abruptly alter target hierarchies. Your model should include “recent slope” features and teammate-availability indicators (from injuries/rosters). 
Sample data pipeline flowchart
mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Ingest
    A[pbp: play-by-play (1999+)]
    B[schedules: lines, totals, rest]
    C[participation: players on field, route, coverage, pressure (2016+)]
    D[nextgen: weekly receiving & passing metrics (2016+)]
    E[injuries: practice status & game status (2009+)]
    F[snap counts (2012+)]
    G[weather: NOAA hourly station data]
  end


  subgraph Build
    H[Normalize IDs: gsis_id/player ids, game_id, play_id]
    I[Create pregame feature windows: last 3/8 games, season-to-date]
    J[Engineer features: expected targets, WOPR-like opportunity, matchup & script]
    K[Label: yards and exceed/meet/fall-below vs rolling baseline]
  end


  subgraph Model
    L[Time-based split: rolling-origin CV + holdout seasons]
    M[Train: boosted trees for accuracy + feature ranking]
    N[Distill: single large tree with pruning for interpretability]
    O[Explain: SHAP + permutation + stability checks]
  end


  subgraph Deploy
    P[Weekly scoring: pregame features -> predicted yards & class probs]
    Q[Monitoring: drift, missingness, recalibration, retrain cadence]
  end

  B --> H
  C --> H
  D --> H
  E --> H
  F --> H
  G --> H
  H --> I --> J --> K --> L --> M --> N --> O --> P --> Q

title: Descicion Tree Part 3
content:
﻿improve WR-yard forecasts by modeling (a) where and how a QB prefers to throw (left/middle/right; short/deep; air-yards profile) and (b) where and how a defense tends to concede throws and yards by direction and coverage. I only use pregame features by shifting every tendency to the last known game/week, then stabilizing noisy rates with rolling windows + empirical-Bayes shrinkage (k≈50). The highest uplift usually comes from “fit” features like (QB tendency − DEF allowance) × WR archetype, because they explicitly encode matchup compatibility instead of treating QB/DEF signals independently. Public coverage/pressure/time-to-throw signals are available via participation data, pass-direction fields are in play-by-play, and 2016+ weekly passing/receiving aggregates are available via Next Gen Stats loaders. 
Variables to add
Below I rank additions by expected uplift for player–game WR yards (2016–present, pregame only). I assume WR archetype inputs (route mix / aDOT / alignment proxies) come from your existing pipeline; if your archetype source is different, that is unspecified and you should map accordingly.
High uplift
* QB pass-direction rates (L/M/R) + efficiency by direction (mechanism: target availability and completion quality differ by side). Transforms: EWMA(8 games, half-life≈3) for rate; EB shrink to league mean; opponent-adjusted delta (qbL − defAllowL); interact with WR “left-role” (alignment/route mix). Availability: public via pbp pass_location (play-level). 
* QB depth profile (short/deep rate; air_yards distribution; aDOT) (mechanism: drives yard variance; deep volume dominates “greatly exceed”). Transforms: short/deep from pass_length; air_yards mean/quantiles; delta vs defense deep allowance; interact with WR deep-route share / aDOT archetype. Availability: public pbp (pass_length, air_yards). 
* Defense direction allowance (targets, yards/att, EPA/att by L/M/R; deep vs short allowed) (mechanism: some defenses funnel throws inside/outside or cap deep shots). Transforms: rolling allowed rates, EB shrink; “funnel” index = allowM − leagueM; interact with QB direction. Availability: public from pbp aggregates by defensive team. 
* Coverage tendencies (man/zone; coverage shell) + pressure context (mechanism: coverage impacts route success; pressure impacts time for deeper concepts). Transforms: rolling def man rate, shell rates; pressure rate; create mismatch terms with QB time-to-throw and WR depth archetype. Availability: public/limited via participation fields defense_man_zone_type, defense_coverage_type, was_pressure, time_to_throw (play-level). 
Medium uplift
* QB “under pressure” adjustments (mechanism: some QBs shift direction/depth when pressured). Transforms: compute QB dir/depth conditional on was_pressure; interact with opponent pressure rate; EB shrink heavily (small n). Availability: participation. 
* Next Gen weekly QB accuracy difficulty-adjusted metrics (e.g., CPOE proxies) (mechanism: stabilizes catchable volume, especially for deep/outside). Transforms: weekly rolling QB CPOE/catchability; interact with WR aDOT; adjust for opponent coverage/pressure. Availability: public but thresholded; 2016+ weekly via load_nextgen_stats(). 
* Starter confirmation + QB change flags (mechanism: first starts and midweek QB changes break historical tendencies). Transforms: “new QB start” indicator; “first 2 starts” bucket. Availability: official gamebooks + schedules; pregame-only if timestamped at kickoff. 
Low uplift (usually situational)
* Very granular coverage-shell splits by direction (mechanism: real, but sparse). Transforms: only keep coarse buckets (MOFC vs MOFO; Cover-1/3 vs 2-high) and EB shrink aggressively. Availability: participation; may vary by season/provider. 
Integration notes (no leakage)
* Joins: pbp uses game_id+play_id; QB identity via passer IDs; defense via defensive team on play; weekly joins by season+week+player ID. 
* Temporal shifting: compute all QB/DEF tendencies from games strictly before the target game (e.g., last game date < current game date; or week-1 within season).
* Missingness: participation and NGS are not “complete for all players/plays;” store missingness flags as features (and never impute as 0). 
Pitfalls
* Collinearity (direction rate, depth rate, air_yards/aDOT all correlate): prefer “fit deltas” and keep base tendencies but prune redundant variants.
* Small-sample noise (rookie QBs / new play-callers / rare shells): EB shrink and minimum-attempt thresholds.
* Schema/provider drift across seasons for participation: keep season (and optionally provider era) as a stabilizer feature. 
Legal/licensing concerns + proxies
* Participation data has explicit attribution/licensing notes (NGS pre-2023; FTN from 2023+)—honor CC-BY-SA requirements and attribution tooling in your pipeline. 
* PFF: consumer subscriptions do not include API access; enterprise access requires separate arrangements. Use only within permitted terms. 
* “Official” real-time data feeds are commercially distributed via partners (e.g., Genius Sports; historical partnerships with Sportradar are reported). If you buy feeds, ensure redistribution rights are explicit. 
* Practical public proxies when ideal coverage tags are unavailable: infer funnel from pbp pass_location/air_yards allowed; infer pressure from sacks/hits/quick throws; infer shell coarsely from depth + middle-field patterns (lower fidelity). 
QB×defense interaction features

Feature-engineering recipes
Below are 12 “drop-in” recipes I use (SQL-ish pseudocode). I treat k=50 as the EB prior sample size; tune by minimizing validation MAE.
sql
Copy
-- helpers
EB(rate,n,mu,k=50) = (rate*n + mu*k)/(n+k)
EWMA(x,halflife=3,last8games)  -- compute on prior games only


1 qbL = EB(EWMA(att_L/att_all), n_att, leagueL)
2 defAL = EB(EWMA(allowAtt_L/allowAtt_all), n_allowAtt, leagueL)
3 fitL_WR = (qbL - defAL) * WR_left_role   -- archetype weight


4 qbDeep = EB(EWMA(att_deep/att_pass), n_att, leagueDeep)
5 defADeep = EB(EWMA(allow_deep/allow_pass), n_allow, leagueDeep)
6 fitDeep_WR = (qbDeep - defADeep) * WR_deep_role


7 qbADOT = EB(EWMA(avg(air_yards)), n_targets, leagueADOT)
8 defAADOT = EB(EWMA(avg_allow_air_yards)), n_allowTargets, leagueADOT)
9 fitADOT_WR = (qbADOT - defAADOT) * WR_aDOT


10 defP = EB(EWMA(pressure_plays/dropbacks), n_dropbacks, leagueP)
11 qbTTT = EB(EWMA(avg(time_to_throw)), n_dropbacks, leagueTTT)
12 rushMismatch = z(qbTTT) * z(defP) * WR_deep_role


Data sources for pass_location/pass_length/air_yards (pbp) and time_to_throw/was_pressure/coverage (participation) are documented in the nflverse ecosystem. 
Validation experiments and metrics
I validate uplift with time-aware backtesting (rolling-origin or “train on seasons ≤Y, test on Y+1”), then report medians across folds. I run ablations: baseline → +QB-only → +DEF-only → +QB×DEF fit terms → +WR-weighted fit terms, using MAE/RMSE for yards and macro-F1 (or balanced accuracy) for exceed/meet/fall-below classes. I expect small but real average gains from QB/DEF fit (often “medium” effect), but the biggest value is usually fewer catastrophic misses for deep WR archetypes and in games with extreme funnels/pressure. For subgroup tests I isolate: rookie QBs (first 6–8 starts), “first start with new team,” man-heavy vs zone-heavy defenses, and deep-aDOT WRs; I aim for ≥2k–5k WR-games per subgroup to reduce noise, otherwise I widen EB priors and treat results as directional. 
Production integration checklist
* ID crosswalks: normalize QB/WR IDs across pbp, participation, NGS weekly, gamebooks (store canonical player IDs; keep name-cleaning logs). 
* Timestamping: store feature snapshots “as-of kickoff” and block any post-kick updates; persist raw extracts for replay.
* Strict shifting: enforce feature_game_date < target_game_date (or week-1) and unit-test it.
* Missingness flags: separate “true zero” from “not recorded” (NGS thresholds; participation provider gaps). 
* Retrain cadence: weekly incremental update + quarterly re-tuning of k and window sizes; re-calibrate classification thresholds each season.
* Drift monitoring: track distribution shifts in coverage labels, pressure rates, and QB direction rates; alert on changes by season/provider. 
Data acquisition list
* nflreadr pbp (free; low effort): pass direction (pass_location), depth (pass_length), air_yards for QB/DEF direction/depth tendencies (play-level). 
* nflreadr participation (free; medium effort): route, defense_man_zone_type, defense_coverage_type, time_to_throw, was_pressure (play-level); mind licensing/attribution notes. 
* Next Gen Stats weekly via nflreadr (free; low effort): QB passing/WR receiving weekly stats (2016+; thresholded); use for stabilized QB accuracy/difficulty proxies. 
* Official gamebooks (free; medium effort): starter/inactive confirmation and metadata (game-level PDFs). 
* PFF coverage/matchup data (paid; medium–high effort): richer man/zone matchup and separation context (2019+ coverage dataset); enterprise access for API-like pulls. 
* Official data distributors (high cost; high effort): real-time official data/odds ecosystems via Genius/Sportradar partnerships (contract-dependent). 
QB/defense feature-join flow
pbp plays: pass_location/pass_length/air_yards
QB rolling + EB
DEF allow rolling + EB
participation: coverage/pressure/TTT
NGS weekly passing
WR archetype: route mix/aDOT/align
QB×DEF fit × WR weights
player-game table
model train: time-aware CV
pregame scoring

title: Udss
content:
﻿Universal Player Prop Pre-Bet Checklist
(UDSS v3.2)
A single template you can run for any player and any prop type (rushing, receiving, passing,
combos, TDs, tackles, kicking, etc.). Fill the core sections, then complete the relevant
module(s) for the prop you’re betting.
1. Intake (Auto-Gather First)
● Player: ______________________
● Prop & Line: ______________________ (e.g., Receiving Yards — 42.5)
● Week / Date / Kickoff: ______________________
● Position: QB / RB / WR / TE / K / DEF (IDP)
● Team (Offense): ______________________
● Opponent: ______________________
● Home/Away: Home ☐ Away ☐
● Stadium/Surface: Dome ☐ Open ☐ Turf ☐ Grass ☐
● Weather (temp / wind / precip): ____° / ____ mph / ____

Quick Intake Script (paste into your assistant to auto-fill):
"Collect position, team, opponent (next game/date), home/away,
stadium/surface, weather, current best line + juice + alt lines, line movement
(24h/72h), player status/practice log, snap/route/attempts last 3–5, season &
last-5 stats vs the line (avg/median), relevant splits (home/away, favored/dog,
man/zone, scheme), O-line vs front-7, and defense vs the prop type."
   1. Market Context
● Best line & book: __________________ (price: ___)
● Consensus line: ______
● Alt lines: __________________ (e.g., 37.5 / 47.5)
● Movement (24h / 72h): ____ / ____
● Market shape: Flat ☐ Split ☐ Steam w/ you ☐ Steam vs you ☐
● News driver present? Yes ☐ No ☐ Notes: __________________________

   2. Player Availability & Role
● Game status: Full ☐ Limited ☐ Q ☐ D ☐ Out ☐
● Practice log (W/Th/F or equiv): ___ / ___ / ___
● Recent snap share (G-1/G-2/G-3): ____% / ____% / ____%
● Role today (check all): Early-downs ☐ Passing-downs ☐ Goal-line ☐ Slot ☐ X/Z ☐
In-line TE ☐ Primary Kicker ☐ LB1 ☐ Edge ☐ CB1 ☐ S ☐
● Depth chart changes / call-ups: ______________________________________
● Coach/beat notes (quotes, plans): ____________________________________

   3. Production vs Line (Hit Rates)
● Season: Avg ____ Median ____ StDev ____ | Over line: ____ / ____ (%)
● Last 5: Avg ____ Median ____ Trend (↑/↓/flat): ____ | Over: ____ / 5 (%)
● Opponent history (2y): G ____ | Avg ____ | High ____ | Low ____
● Key splits: Home ____ / Away ____ | Fav ____ / Dog ____ | Man ____ / Zone ____ |
Pace up/down ____

   4. Usage & Efficiency (Universal)
● Snaps/game: Season ____ | L5 ____
● Routes/game (for WR/TE/RB/QB): Season ____ | L5 ____
● Team share: Target % ____ | Rush share ____ | Air-yard share ____
● Efficiency: YPRR ____ | YAC/rec ____ | aDOT ____ | YPC ____ | EPA/play ____
● High-leverage: RZ touches ____ | Inside-5 opps ____ | 3rd/4th-down role ____
● Volatility flags: Boom-bust ☐ Low volume ☐ Committee ☐

Offensive Line & Protection
● Run-block win rate / grade: __________
● Pass-block win rate / pressure allowed (PBWR / Press%): __________
● OL injuries/returns: _________________________________________________
● ΔT2T (QB time-to-throw delta vs season): ______ ms
● OL continuity (healthy starters active): ______ / 5
● PBWR_adj gap vs Opp Pressure%: ______ (if Opp Pressure% − PBWR_adj ≥ 8% → see Hard Gates)
      5. Opponent Defense — Choose the Module(s)
Complete the module(s) that match the prop type you’re betting.

A) RUSH Module (RB/WR/QB rushing yards/attempts/TDs)
● Defense vs run: Rush yards/gm ____ | YPC ____ | EPA/rush ____ | Success% ____
● Explosive run rate allowed (10+): ____% | Stuff rate: ____%
● Front-7 health / run D rank (DVOA/PFF): __________
● Box counts (stacked %): ____% | Missed tackle% (run): ____%
● Scheme match (wide zone / gap) vs defense strengths: __________________
● Offense run scheme: Outside-zone ____% | Gap/Power ____%
● Defense vs OZ rank ____ | vs Gap/Power rank ____
● QB rush environment: Spy rate ____% | Zone ____% | Contain rank/top-10? ☐ | Designed QB runs ≥2/gm? ____
→ If Spy ≥12% OR Zone ≥65% with top-10 contain AND designed runs <2/gm → see Hard Gates
B) RECEIVE Module (WR/TE/RB receiving yards/receptions/targets/TDs)
● Coverage & scheme: Man ____% | Zone ____% | Press ____% | Blitz ____%
● Defense vs position: Yds/gm to WR ____ / TE ____ / RB ____ | Y/Tgt ____
● Explosive pass rate allowed: ____% | YAC allowed: ____ | MT%: ____%
● Primary matchup(s): CB/S/LB __________________ | Shadow risk: High ☐
● Pass rush pressure rate: ____% (affects aDOT/time to throw)
● CB room rank (top-10? ☐) | Press/Man ≥30%? ____% / ____%
● Bracket/double rate on primary (Kelce/WR1): ____% (cap targets/aDOT if ≥35%)
● Checkdown funnel signal (pressure high + WRs locked): RB target uplift? Yes ☐ No ☐
● Offense pass style: PA deep-shot ____% | RPO/quick ____% | Empty ____% | Motion ____% | 12/13 personnel ____%
● Defense counters: Sim/Creeper ____% | Match-Quarters/Robber ____% | Press/Man ____% | Blitz vs Empty ____%
C) PASS Module (QB passing yards/attempts/completions/TD/INT)
● Defense vs pass: EPA/db ____ | Success% ____ | Explosive pass% ____
● Pressure / blitz: ____% / ____% | P2S%: ____% | Man/zone rates: /
● INT rate: ____% | Coverage busts big-play%: ____%
● OL pass-pro vs opp front (edge/DT health): ____________________________
● ΔT2T vs season: ____ ms | Spy ____% | Contain rank/top-10? ☐ | Zone ____%
● Offense pass style: PA deep-shot ____% | RPO/quick ____% | Empty ____% | Motion ____% | 12/13 personnel ____%
● Defense counters: Sim/Creeper ____% | Match-Quarters/Robber ____% | Press/Man ____% | Blitz vs Empty ____%
D) KICK Module (FG made/longest, PATs)
● Team RZ TD% vs FG%: ____ / ____
● Coach 4th-down aggressiveness: High ☐ Med ☐ Low ☐
● Venue/weather: Dome ☐ Open ☐ | Wind ____ mph | Turf/grass ______
● Kicker profiles: Career 50+% ____ | Accuracy ____% | Recent form ____
● Opponent ST block/pressure rates: __________________________
● 4th-down go-rate (last 3 / season): ____% / ____%
● RZ TD finish rate (last 3 / season): ____% / ____%
→ High go-rate + high RZ TD% = fade FGM overs; PAT/TD environments = boom/bust
E) DEFENSE/IDP Module (tackles+assists, sacks, PDs)
● Player role: LB1 ☐ LB2 ☐ Edge ☐ Box S ☐ Slot CB ☐ Outside CB ☐
● Alignment (box% / slot% / deep%): ____ / ____ / ____
● Team snap expectation: Pace ____ | Opponent plays/gm ____ | Run rate ____%
● Tackle share / avg depth of tackle: ____% / ____ yds
● Opponent run/pass tendency and completion%: ____ / ____ | ____%
         6. Game Environment
● Spread / Total: ______ / ______ | Implied Team Total: ______
● Likely script for your prop: Supports ☐ Neutral ☐ Opposes ☐ Why: ______
● Rest / travel / short week: ___________________________________________

         7. Red Flags (Critical Overrides)
● ☐ Snap cap (return from injury/conditioning)
● ☐ Late downgrade / DNP
● ☐ OL injuries materially change outlook
● ☐ Severe weather for the prop type (wind/rain for passing/kicking; field for rushing)
● ☐ Game-script risk opposite of prop
● ☐ Market steam against (≥2.5 yards or ≥20¢) without new info
● ☐ Role change / committee shift
● ☐ Key teammate injury changes defensive attention (e.g., WR1 out)

Hard Gates (Auto-PASS or Re-route)
● Coverage/Protection Gate: (Opp Pressure% − PBWR_adj) ≥ 8% AND CB room top-10 AND press/man ≥30% → PASS WR/QB overs; TE only if no bracket; upgrade RB receiving.
● Primary Receiver Constraint: Bracket ≥35% AND ΔT2T ≤ −80ms → Reduce target share −4–8 pts and cap aDOT −1.0–1.5; many WR/TE overs become PASS.
● QB Rush/Contain Gate: Spy ≥12% OR (Zone ≥65% + top-10 contain) → Scramble expectation −20–35%; fade QB rush overs unless designed runs ≥2/gm.
● OL Continuity Gate: ≤3 healthy starters → PASS QB/WR overs; TE only if not held in protection.
● Checkdown Funnel Rule: Gate hits + CB lock → Upgrade RB receiving overs; downshift outside-WR deep/long.
● Kicker Philosophy Gate: High go-rate + high RZ TD% → Fade FGM overs; TD props = boom/bust.
● Injury Contingency: If X exits → Y benefits/Z loses (pre-write redistribution trees).
● Scheme Collision Gate: (auto-PASS or heavy downgrade)
• Trigger (any 1 strong or 2 medium):
– Run: Offense outside-zone ≥55% vs defense top-8 vs outside-zone OR offense gap/power ≥55% vs defense top-8 vs gap/power.
– Pass—Vertical/Deep PA: Offense PA/deep-shot rate ≥30% with T2T ≥2.8s vs defense sim/creeper ≥20% + press/man ≥30% + pressure ≥ league avg.
– Pass—RPO/Quick: Offense RPO/quick-game ≥45% vs defense match-quarters/robber ≥30% + squat/press ≥30%.
– Empty: Offense empty ≥15% vs defense blitz/sim vs empty ≥35%.
• Action:
– RB rush overs: −10–20% expectation if run collision; auto-PASS if PBWR_adj gap is also bad.
– WR/TE overs: cap aDOT −0.8–1.2; target share −3–6 pts if vertical-PA collision; many become PASS.
– QB pass overs: reduce explosive% and yards; lean UNDER/PASS.
– QB rush overs: if vertical collision + contain/spy present, treat as QB Rush/Contain Gate.
– RB receiving: upgrade when pass collisions imply checkdown funnel.
            8. UDSS Scoring (Universal)
0–2 ✅ SAFE | 3–5 ⚠ RISKY | 6+ ❌ DANGEROUS. Any red-flag override = ❌
A. Form vs Line (0–3 pts)
● Season median vs line: ≥ line ☐ (0) | 0.5–2 under ☐ (+1) | ≥2.5 under ☐ (+2)
● Last-5 median vs line: ≥ line ☐ (0) | 0.5–2 under ☐ (+1) | ≥2.5 under ☐ (+2)
● Volume stability (snaps/routes/attempts): Stable ☐ (0) | Variable ☐ (+1)
B. Role & Leverage (0–3 pts)
● Primary role ☐ (0) | Split ☐ (+1) | Secondary ☐ (+2)
● High-leverage share (RZ/inside-5/1st-read): Strong ☐ (0) | Mixed ☐ (+1) | Weak ☐ (+2)
C. Matchup Fit (0–3 pts)
● Defense vs prop type: Favorable ☐ (0) | Neutral ☐ (+1) | Tough ☐ (+2)
● OL vs Front-7 or Coverage fit: Edge O ☐ (0) | Even ☐ (+1) | Edge D ☐ (+2)
D. Environment & Market (0–2 pts)
● Script/weather supportive and no steam vs you: Yes ☐ (0) | Mixed ☐ (+1) | Bad ☐ (+2)
Total UDSS Score: ______ → Rating: SAFE ☐ RISKY ☐ DANGEROUS ☐
Note: Apply Hard Gates and Red Flags first; any gate/override = PASS regardless of score.

            9. Final Call
● Pick: Higher ☐ Lower ☐ Pass ☐
● Unit size (0.25u–1u): ______
● Reasoning (1–2 lines): ______________________________________________
● Correlated angles (SGP/hedges): ______________________________________

            10. One-Minute Quick Pass (Universal)

               1. Role & volume today (snaps/routes/attempts primary?)

               2. Medians vs line (season & last-5)

               3. Defense vs prop type (fit + OL/coverage)

               4. Script & weather (supportive?)

               5. Market (no sharp steam against?)

               6. Protection vs Coverage collision? (Opp Pressure% − PBWR_adj ≥8% and elite CBs press/man ≥30%)

               7. Bracket ≥35% with ΔT2T ≤ −80ms? QB spy/contain ≥ thresholds?

               8. Scheme collision present (run or pass)? If yes → downgrade/PASS.

                  11. Data Dictionary (Universal)
● YPRR / aDOT / YAC: Receiving efficiency & depth indicators.
● EPA/play / Success rate: Drive-sustaining efficiency; context for matchup fit.
● Explosive rate: % plays over big-gain thresholds (10+ rush, 15+ pass).
● Run-/Pass-block win rate: OL vs DL ability to create/hold lanes.
● Pressure/Blitz/P2S%: Pass rush environment affecting QBs/WRs/TEs.
● Tackle share/ADT: IDP volume and role indicators.
● Market steam: Rapid, multi-book moves often tied to fresh info.
● ΔT2T: Change in QB time-to-throw vs season baseline (ms); negative means faster decisions/shorter routes.
● PBWR_adj: Pass-block win rate adjusted for opponent strength and chip/help rate.
● Bracket rate: % snaps primary pass-catcher is doubled/bracketed.
● Spy rate / Contain rank: % snaps defense assigns QB spy; contain quality rank (top-10 indicates strong edge discipline).
● Checkdown funnel: Pressure + coverage combo that shifts targets from WRs to RBs (screens, flats, arrows).
● Scheme collision: Structural mismatch between core offensive concepts (OZ/gap, deep PA, RPO/quick, empty) and defense counters (top run fits, sim/creeper, match-quarters/robber, blitz vs empty).

How to Use (examples)
“Run UDSS for James Cook — Rushing Yards 12.5 vs Dolphins.” → Fill RUSH module.
“Run UDSS for Khalil Shakir — Receptions 3.5 vs Dolphins.” → Fill RECEIVE module.
“Run UDSS for Josh Allen — Pass Yards 233.5 vs Dolphins.” → Fill PASS module.
“Run UDSS for Kicker X — FGs Made 1.5.” → Fill KICK module.
Tip: Duplicate this sheet per pick or print it.
